documents.019e1de0-6b2a-71be-8361-939b60b0739a:
# Manual underwriting can't scale. Agentic underwriting can.

AI agents that intake, enrich, score, and monitor across the funding lifecycle.
documents.019e1de0-7249-7028-9e58-311ebb35f57b:
# underscore

API-first agentic and ML risk scoring for small business portfolios. Send a deal, get back enriched analysis and a risk score in seconds.

**Tag:** Risk Scoring\
**Icon:** S

## How underscore works

A fund sends a deal via API. Bank statements, application data, and existing positions are ingested and parsed automatically. ML models trained on deal outcomes score the applicant across revenue patterns, existing positions, and anomaly signals. The risk score is returned in seconds.

### Continuous monitoring

underscore does not stop at origination. Every active deal is re-underwritten on a continuous basis. Pre-default signals are flagged automatically, not discovered at collections. Portfolio-level exposure dashboards give fund managers a real-time view of risk concentration.

### API-first integration

RESTful API with Python and Node.js SDKs. Score deals from your existing workflow tools. Webhook notifications for portfolio events. Built for engineering teams that want scoring infrastructure, not another dashboard.
documents.019e1de0-79bd-7329-9444-cba3f516f0af:
# underchat agent

Brand-matched AI agents that engage your clients 24/7. Guardrailed, observable agent personas handle intake, RFIs, document collection, recovery, and renewals across web chat, email, SMS, and Slack, resolving 80% of cases autonomously and routing the rest to humans with full context.

**Tag:** Client Engagement\
**Icon:** U

## How underchat agent works

The same agent layer extends frontline teams across the client lifecycle: a minimum 5x throughput uplift without adding headcount. Every conversation is on-brand, observable, and auditable, so humans review only the cases the agent flags.

### Multi-channel coverage

One agent persona, every channel. Web chat, email, SMS, and Slack are handled by the same brand-matched agent with shared context. Conversations move between channels without losing thread, and every interaction is logged for review.

### Guardrailed autonomy

Agents resolve 80% of cases autonomously and route the rest to humans with full context. Guardrails enforce on-brand voice, scope boundaries, and escalation triggers. Every action is observable and auditable, so humans review only the cases the agent flags.
documents.019e1de0-81ca-708f-ae41-b584090e33ff:
# underchat co-pilot

Hybrid ML and agentic scoring that risk-grades your deal book with institutional-grade accuracy — interactive, conversational, and built to unlock yield from every position you hold.

**Tag:** Co-Pilot\
**Icon:** C

## Not a workflow tool

underchat co-pilot is not another queue, ticketing system, or pipeline dashboard. It is an agent orchestration platform that risk-grades deals. You interact with it the same way you interact with a senior analyst: you ask questions, you direct attention, you approve actions. The chat surface is the interface — your portfolio, your scorecards, and your decisions live inside the conversation, with the underlying analysis, evidence, and orchestration handled by the co-pilot.

## Hybrid scoring

Hybrid ML and agentic scoring is the core of underchat co-pilot. A statistical baseline trained on your wins and losses provides the quantitative floor. An agentic reasoning layer interrogates the structured and unstructured evidence — bank statements, AR/AP, communications, RFI responses — and adjusts the score with cited reasoning. The result is a single grade per deal with a full evidentiary trace: every component, every assumption, every source document.

### Superior accuracy

The hybrid approach outperforms rules-based scorecards and pure-ML systems because each layer covers the other's blind spots. ML catches base-rate patterns humans miss. Agentic reasoning catches the situational nuances that wreck purely statistical models.

### Institutional-grade discipline

Every score is reproducible, traceable, and auditable. Reasoning is cited, evidence is linked, and the scorecard is defensible to your investment committee, your back-end capital partners, and your auditors.

### Learns from your book

Outcomes from your closed, renewed, and unwound deals feed back into the scoring layer. The co-pilot sharpens with every decision your team makes.

## Yield

Hybrid scoring unlocks yield from positions you already hold and from positions you haven't yet seen. Re-underwrite live positions to surface under-priced risk. Evaluate deals for secondary sale with institutional rigor. Roll out new underwriting guidelines across the team in days, not quarters.

### Re-underwrite the book

Pass your existing deal book through the co-pilot and surface positions where current pricing has drifted from current risk. Reprice with confidence, with a documented trail.

### Secondary sale

Score deals for a buyer that doesn't know your book the way you do. Produce institutional-grade tape, defensible scorecards, and the evidence package they'll ask for.

### Guideline rollout

New underwriting guidelines that used to take a quarter to roll out can land in days. The co-pilot applies them consistently across the team and surfaces the deals that flip under the new rules.

## How you interact

You talk to your co-pilot. From the chat surface inside your team's tools — or by email, Slack, or SMS — you ask questions, direct attention, and approve actions. "Look into this deal." "Why did this score drop?" "Send the AR concentration RFI." The co-pilot ingests, reasons, scores, drafts, and waits for your approval before taking any consequential action.

## Workflow

1. Ingest — multimodal data from Plaid, bank statements, QuickBooks, AR/AP, cashflow, bank letters, and RFI responses.
2. Score — hybrid ML and agentic scoring produces an institutional-grade risk grade with cited evidence.
3. Decide — the co-pilot recommends; you approve. Screening, autodecline, offers, renewals, and recovery routing.
4. Orchestrate — RFIs, approvals, and updates flow through the channels your team already uses, under strict compliance guardrails.
5. Observe — portfolio analytics, custom reports, and agent-surfaced insights — all available on demand.

## Integrations

Connects to your stack. Plaid, QuickBooks, Salesforce, Gmail, and Slack as core integrations. Plus bank statement PDFs, AR/AP statements, CSV/Sheets, webhooks, and REST API.

## Guardrails

Human-in-the-loop, by design. The co-pilot proposes; underwriters approve, trigger, and override. Approval gates on every consequential action. Every claim cites evidence. Every decision is reproducible. End-to-end audit trail.
documents.019e1de0-9dbc-70af-9728-5dd3849152a7:
**Date:** 2025-11-20\
**Excerpt:** The role of AI in private credit is augmentation, not automation.\
**Tag:** Insight\
**Author:** Sajit Roshan

---

The conversation around AI in underwriting often gets framed as replacement. Will machines take over? The answer is no, and here is why.

Private credit underwriting requires judgment that cannot be reduced to a formula. What AI does is remove the drudge work: data gathering, initial screening, precedent lookup. The underwriter still makes the call.

The best analogy is spell-check for writers. No one argues that spell-check replaced authors. It freed them to focus on craft. AI underwriting tools do the same for risk professionals.

undersight built underchat with this philosophy. Every feature exists to make the underwriter faster and more consistent, not to replace their judgment. The copilot surfaces what matters. The human decides.
documents.019e1de0-aa70-72e2-a186-f94e95fde206:
**Date:** 2026-02-18\
**Excerpt:** Client intake is the slowest link in the submission chain. Here is how to fix it.\
**Tag:** Insight\
**Author:** Sajit Roshan

---

Every funder knows the pain: incomplete submissions, back-and-forth emails, missing bank statements. The RFI process is where deals stall and brokers lose patience.

Agentic Client RFI attacks this directly. Instead of static forms and email chains, an AI agent conducts an intelligent conversation with the broker, gathering exactly what is needed, validating as it goes, and producing a clean structured submission.

The result: funder-ready packages assembled from raw inputs. No manual follow-up. Zero back-and-forth. Brokers get a better experience, and underwriters get complete files.
documents.019e1de0-b95d-71f6-aab6-aecc7c21e347:
**Date:** 2026-03-12\
**Excerpt:** Lessons learned building AI assistants for institutional underwriting teams.\
**Tag:** Insight\
**Author:** Sajit Roshan

---

Building a copilot for underwriters is not the same as building a chatbot. Underwriters need precision, not conversation. They need relevant precedents surfaced at the right moment, not generic summaries.

underchat Co-pilot was designed around the actual workflow: review submission, assess exposure, check precedent, price, decide. Every feature maps to a real step in that process.

documents.019e1f07-df81-73d5-9051-50ca674c1a9d:
**Date:** 2026-05-08\
**Excerpt:** A deal auto-declined by rules-based underwriting gets funded in under 5 minutes through agentic intake and pre-underwriting.\
**Tag:** Case Study

---

# How Chat Advance funded a declined deal in 5 minutes

Chat Advance is a funding marketplace built on top of undersight. When a traditional rules-based underwriter auto-declined a small business applicant, the undersight platform told a different story.

## The setup

A small business owner needed $30K for 60 days. They had reliable but lumpy revenue from state contracts. On paper, the deal didn't fit standard criteria. A rules-based system declined it instantly.

But Chat Advance runs on undersight. Instead of a static application form, the applicant connected with Macy, the AI intake agent.

## What happened next

### Step 1: The applicant engages via chat

Macy guided the business owner through the process, answering questions about revenue-based financing along the way. No forms, no email chains. A single conversation.

### Step 2: Bank data ingested and enriched

Three months of bank statements were uploaded via Plaid. underchat enriched the data automatically. Transactions categorized, revenue patterns identified, anomalies flagged for verification.

### Step 3: RFIs addressed with the applicant

Macy identified potential underwriter questions about the bank statement anomalies and addressed them directly with the applicant. No human reviewer needed for the initial pass.

### Step 4: Matching funders receive a deal submission

Funders matching the deal criteria received a rich submission package including preliminary RFI responses and deal analysis. Two funders matched. Two offers extended.

## The result

The applicant received two funding offers within five minutes of starting the conversation:

* Offer 1: $100K, 26 weeks, 1.25x factor
* Offer 2: $80K, 34 weeks, 1.34x factor

A deal that was auto-declined by conventional screening got funded because agentic intake captured what a rules engine couldn't see.

## Why this matters

This is not about replacing underwriters. It's about creating a path for deals that would otherwise never get evaluated. The undersight stack handled intake, enrichment, RFI resolution, and funder matching end-to-end. The human-in-the-loop approved and closed.

### By the numbers

* **24/7** availability for applicants
* **Ultra-low** customer acquisition cost
* **60-second** screening
* **Personalized** direct engagement

Chat Advance is one example. The same agentic workflow runs for funds, brokers, and marketplaces across the undersight platform.
documents.019e22a9-2522-7174-9582-18beb1996be5:
**Date:** 2025-12-12\
**Excerpt:** A technology-enabled transformation thesis for how undersight helps C/D-rated funds achieve ABS-grade standards and access institutional capital.\
**Tag:** Research\
**Author:** Kyle Adriany\
**Subtitle:** A Technology-Enabled Transformation Thesis

---

# Unlocking Institutional Capital for Mid-Tier MCA Funds

*A Technology-Enabled Transformation Thesis*

**How undersight enables C/D-rated funds to achieve ABS-grade standards**

*For investors and bank CROs evaluating the MCA credit opportunity*

This analysis builds on [Edgar Matthews & Co., "The Institutional Migration of Merchant Cash Advance and Revenue-Based Finance," January 2026](https://www.edgarmatthews.com).

---

## Executive Summary

MCA securitization has matured into a credible institutional asset class. Since 2021, approximately eight platforms have successfully executed rated securitizations, collectively securitizing over $1.1 billion in 2025 alone. These platforms include [Enova (OnDeck)](https://www.enova.com), [Kapitus](https://www.kapitus.com), and [BasePoint](https://www.basepointcapital.com).

However, the vast majority of MCA funds remain structurally locked out of this capital. The barrier is not asset quality or borrower demand. The central limitation is the absence of institutional-grade structure across data, legal enforceability, servicing infrastructure, and portfolio governance.

This represents a significant market dislocation. Institutional capital seeks high-yield access within the $1.34 trillion US private credit market, yet most MCA originators cannot meet the operational standards required for warehouse facilities, let alone rated securitization.

## The Transformation Opportunity

Technology can close this gap. Mid-tier funds that modernize their operational infrastructure along five institutional dimensions can transform from uninvestible to attractive. The Matthews framework identifies these dimensions as:

| Dimension | Weight | Standard |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Data Integrity | 25% | Immutable contracts, clean reconciliation, historical depth |
| Payment Stability | 25% | Automated collections, explainable patterns |
| Servicing Continuity | 20% | Documented processes, personnel resilience |
| Buyback Discipline | 15% | Clear triggers, adequate funding |
| Stacking Controls | 15% | Detection capability, performance differentiation |

undersight is building the infrastructure that enables this transformation. By embedding autonomous underwriting and portfolio monitoring technology, undersight supports four of five dimensions directly.

### Key Market Indicators

* $613M securitized across three 2025 deals ([ONDK](https://www.enova.com), [Kapitus](https://www.kapitus.com), [BasePoint](https://www.basepointcapital.com))
* 21+ total MCA securitizations since 2021
* Class A tranche pricing: T+125 to T+232
* \~8 unique sponsors have achieved institutional access

The question is not whether MCA assets are institutionally viable. The question is how to scale that viability beyond the handful of platforms that have self-funded the transformation.

---

## What Disqualifies Mid-Tier Funds Today

The deficiencies are operational, not economic. Funds fail institutional standards not because assets underperform, but because infrastructure cannot withstand diligence. Key structural weaknesses:

**Legal uncertainty:** Many MCA structures rely on sale-of-receivables doctrine to avoid usury statutes, but courts increasingly apply "function-over-form" analysis. Platforms must be engineered to survive loan re-characterization rather than relying on sale doctrine as a sole line of defense.

**Enforcement fragility:** In MCA, collateral is operational enforcement. The ability to identify disruption, implement remedies, and restore remittance determines recovery more than contractual documentation. Funds without scalable enforcement infrastructure cannot support institutional leverage.

**Informational opacity:** Without granular disruption data, recovery timing, and re-engagement rates, rating agencies cannot differentiate between recoverable operational issues and terminal credit losses.

**Stacking correlation:** When multiple funders underwrite the same revenue stream without centralized coordination, competing daily pulls fragment cash flow across all exposures. Stacking converts portfolio granularity into hidden correlation, rendering institutional tranching mathematically unstable.

---

## Market Potential: Latent Demand for High-Yield Access

### The Yield Arbitrage

MCA assets offer exceptional risk-adjusted returns. Net yields after losses range from 18-25% for well-managed portfolios; institutional private credit targeting small business lending typically achieves 8-12%.

This spread reflects operational intensity and structural complexity, not market inefficiency. For institutional capital willing to deploy through qualifying platforms, the risk-adjusted opportunity is substantial.

Recent securitization pricing demonstrates appetite. Class A tranches in 2025 deals priced between T+125 and T+232:

* ONDK 2025-2 (AA rated): T+125, $261M
* BSPNT 2025-1 (A/A- rated): T+232, $102M
* KAPT 2025-1 (AA- rated): T+185, $250M

### Why Banks Cannot Lend Directly to C/D Funds

Banks face a structural dilemma. High-yield MCA assets fit portfolio allocation mandates, but most originators cannot satisfy credit committee requirements:

1. **Data insufficiency:** Banks require loan-level historical data, reconciliation trails, and static pool analytics. Most mid-tier funds cannot produce this consistently.
2. **Servicing scalability:** Warehouse facilities require third-party servicer ratings or demonstrable operational resilience. Key-person dependencies disqualify platforms.
3. **Legal uncertainty:** Function-over-form risk creates regulatory capital complications.
4. **Stacking correlation:** Without cross-platform visibility, banks cannot assess true portfolio risk.

---

## How undersight Enables the Transformation

### Positioning: Infrastructure, Not Software

undersight does not just sell software to MCA funds. undersight creates the infrastructure that makes institutional capital deployment possible.

The positioning is narrow by design. undersight sits between the fund and the bank, addressing the specific structural deficiencies that prevent capital flow.

### The Three-Layer Value Stack

undersight organizes capabilities into three progressive layers:

**Layer 1: Automate**

* Autonomous underwriting using agentic AI
* Replaces manual document processing, credit data aggregation, deal structuring
* Usage-based pricing scaling with deal volume
* \~$6B addressable market across small business underwriting

**Layer 2: Monitor**

* Continuous portfolio monitoring, payment pattern analysis, disruption detection
* Early warning systems, downside protection
* Progressing toward outcome-based pricing
* \~$0.5-1.5B addressable market

**Layer 3: Unlock**

* Audit-ready reporting, structured data exports, compliance frameworks
* Institutional observability, capital enablement
* \~$0.5-1.0B addressable market

The progression is deliberate. Funds adopt Layer 1 for efficiency. Layer 2 compounds value through performance gains. Layer 3 unlocks the transformation payoff: institutional capital access at materially better terms.

### Mapping to Matthews Dimensions

| Dimension | undersight Layer | Gap Closed |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Data Integrity (25%) | Layer 1: Automate | Complete. Immutable, structured origination data at deal inception |
| Payment Stability (25%) | Layer 2: Monitor | Substantial. Distinguishes business failure from cash-flow disruption |
| Servicing Continuity (20%) | Layer 1: Automate | Complete. Documented, system-driven workflows |
| Buyback Discipline (15%) | Layer 2: Monitor | Complete. Early default prediction, trigger monitoring |
| Stacking Controls (15%) | Layers 1 + 2 | Substantial. Position detection plus ongoing monitoring |

undersight addresses four of five dimensions directly and provides substantial support for the fifth (Payment Stability requires complementary operational improvements that technology alone cannot fully solve).

### Why Agentic AI Matters

Traditional underwriting automation uses rules-based systems or ML models. undersight employs agentic AI with tool access operating within structured workflows. This architecture directly enables institutional readiness:

* **Immutability:** Agentic systems generate audit trails by design. Every decision includes reasoning, data sources, and confidence scoring.
* **Pattern recognition:** Diverse signals (processor changes, bank account updates, geographic patterns) enable accurate disruption classification.
* **Process codification:** The agent's reasoning becomes documentation, eliminating key-person dependencies.

---

## What Needs to Align: The Three-Party Coordination Problem

Transformation requires alignment across the fund, the bank, and the infrastructure provider. The thesis succeeds only when all three coordinate.

### Fund-Side Requirements

**Profile:** Small/mid-tier MCA fund ($25-250M annual originations) seeking competitive differentiation.

**Leadership commitment:** Buy-in for organizational transformation, not merely cost reduction.

**Timeline:** 12-24 month transformation horizon. Data integrity improvements begin immediately; servicing continuity matures over 6-12 months; institutional pitch-readiness emerges in year two.

### Bank-Side Requirements

**Sponsor:** CRO or senior credit officer who defines "institutional-grade" for deployment criteria.

**Engagement:** Feedback during transformation, not pass/fail evaluation at the end.

**The carrot:** Defined capital deployment structure, whether warehouse facility, SPV participation, or direct ABS investment.

### undersight Role

**Not:** A software vendor selling tools.

**Instead:** Infrastructure partner enabling transformation through deep operational integration. The infrastructure must become the operational foundation.

---

## Conclusion

### For Investors

undersight addresses a market dislocation at the intersection of operational infrastructure and institutional capital access.

**The opportunity:** MCA securitization is proven viable. However, the majority of funds remain locked out despite originating attractive yield assets. The first platform to enable transformation at scale captures capital concentration from both fund and bank sides.

**Market size:** $7-8.5B across three layers.

**Traction:** Founded October 2025, product launched November 2025, closed first customer at $100K ARR in December 2025.

### For Banks

Mid-tier MCA funds are underserved, not uninvestible.

**The framework:** [Matthews' five-dimension readiness index](https://www.edgarmatthews.com) provides the evaluation rubric.

**The infrastructure layer:** Technology can close the operational gap. Funds that embed undersight infrastructure generate documentation, analytics, and process evidence that credit committees require.

### Closing

The institutionalization of MCA will not be broad. But for the few platforms that execute correctly, the payoff will be transformational.

undersight is building the infrastructure that makes that execution possible.

---

## Market Size

**US MCA Market: $19-22B annual originations | 7-9% CAGR (2024-2032)**

| Tier | Originations | Funders |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Enterprise ($1B+) | $8-12B | 5-10 |
| Large ($250M-$1B) | $5-8B | 10-20 |
| Mid-Market ($50M-$250M) | $3-6B | 30-50 |
| Small ($10M-$50M) | $1.5-3B | 50-100 |
| Micro (<$10M) | $0.5-1B | 100-150 |
| **Total** | **$19-22B** | **200-300** |

* 1,000-2,000 ISOs/brokers

*Working Paper, Draft 0.1. Author: Kyle Adriany, undersight.*
documents.019e27cc-5434-73d2-81c2-6ba4bdce647b:
**Date:** 2026-01-22\
**Excerpt:** How agentic AI architectures can transform credit assessment from rigid scorecards to adaptive, context-aware evaluation systems.\
**Tag:** Research\
**Author:** Sajit Roshan\
**Subtitle:** A Vision for AI-Native Credit Assessment Architectures

---

# From Deterministic Scorecards to Agentic Credit Assessments

## A Vision for AI-Native Credit Assessment Architectures

### Abstract

Traditional small-business credit underwriting assessments rely on deterministic scorecards: fixed hierarchies of dimensions, metrics, bins, and weights designed ex ante by domain experts. While effective for standardization and governance, such systems fundamentally assume that risk is enumerable, stationary, and linearly aggregable. Such systems also assume that a single scorecard (or a small-set of similar scorecards) can cover all types and sizes of businesses. This paper outlines a research and engineering vision for transitioning from deterministic scorecards to fully agentic, AI-native credit assessments; systems in which not only credit decisions, but the very *structure of evaluation itself*, emerges dynamically at runtime.

## 1. The Deterministic Scorecard as a Necessary but Insufficient Baseline

Deterministic scorecards represent a critical starting point. They encode institutional knowledge, regulatory constraints, and operational consistency through:

* Pre-defined dimensions (e.g., revenue quality, cash flow strength)
* Fixed sub-dimensions (e.g., stability, durability, concentration)
* Deterministic metrics, bins, scores, and hierarchical weights
* Aggregation rules yielding interpretable ratings at each level

This framework establishes a shared ontology of risk, enabling reproducibility, auditability, and alignment across teams. However, this approach also hard-codes assumptions about what constitutes "risk", how risk factors interact, and which signals matter across ALL businesses and regimes. As a result, deterministic scorecards are inherently brittle in the face of the long tail of small businesses, non-stationary environments, and adversarial or strategic behavior.

## 2. Introducing Agentic Assessment Layers Over Deterministic Structure

The first evolutionary step is not replacement, but augmentation. We envision agentic layers that *operate over* the deterministic scorecard, producing structured assessments at both the sub-dimension and dimension levels. These assessments are not mere scores, but reasoned judgments, expressed as a rating, a short natural-language justification, and an associated confidence or uncertainty measure.

Two modes of agentic assessment are possible:

### 2.1 Semi-Agentic Assessment

In this mode, the agent has access to the deterministic scorecard's metrics, binning logic, metric scores, weights and aggregation rules exposed via programmatic interfaces (e.g., MCP-style tools). The agent reasons *within* the scorecard's structure while adding interpretation, contextualization, and cross-metric synthesis. This allows the system to retain strong governance while introducing flexibility and explanation.

### 2.2 Fully-Agentic Assessment

In contrast, fully-agentic assessment removes reliance on predefined metrics, bins and weights. The agent forms independent judgments about pre-defined sub-dimensions and dimensions based on observed evidence, learned patterns, and contextual knowledge. The deterministic scorecard becomes a reference point rather than an execution constraint.

This marks the transition from *rule-following evaluation* to *model-based reasoning*.

Introducing agentic assessment layers over deterministic scorecards naturally enables agent specialization and composable decision architectures that are impossible in monolithic scoring systems. Rather than a single model producing a unified score, multiple specialized agents can be assigned distinct cognitive roles: domain-expert underwriting agents focusing on specific scorecard dimensions (e.g., revenue behavior, leverage, cash-flow resilience), agents dedicated to metric interpretation (such as binning or normalization), and agents responsible for weighting, aggregation, or uncertainty estimation. These agents can be organized in different architectural patterns such as a risk-committee configuration, where two or three expert underwriting agents deliberate in parallel and a deliberately contrarian agent challenges consensus assumptions; or a sequential expert pipeline, where reasoning is progressively refined and stress-tested across stages. Such agentic architectures mirror real-world credit committees while remaining fully programmable, allowing institutions to encode diversity of perspective, adversarial reasoning, and domain specialization directly into the underwriting system, rather than relying on a single aggregated model or opaque score.

## 3. Agentic Construction of the Scorecard Itself

The next - and more radical - stage is to treat the scorecard not as a static artifact, but as a *generated object*. Under this paradigm dimensions, sub-dimensions, and metrics are not fixed ex ante. Each deal results in a *bespoke evaluative structure*, and the "scorecard" becomes an emergent representation of how the system understands that specific business as well as the quality and the quantity of data available for underwriting.

Here, underwriting is no longer the act of populating a predefined schema. Instead, it is the act of both constructing an appropriate scorecard as well as conducting a credit assessment using that carefully crafted scorecard.

An agentic construction of the scorecard is not a nicety, nor an attempt to force agentic thinking onto an existing framework. It is a necessity in small-business credit assessment because the underlying evaluation problem is inherently non-uniform and context-dependent. Small businesses vary dramatically in size, industry, operating cadence, and data exhaust - ranging from short bank statement histories to multi-year records, and from clean financials to partial or idiosyncratic documents such as AR-aging reports or irregular statements. In such settings, pre-defining deterministic scorecards for all permutations is neither practical nor epistemically sound: even basic statistical constructs (e.g., standard deviation) may be meaningless given limited history, while aggregation choices (daily vs. weekly vs. monthly inflows) must adapt to data length and business rhythm. Moreover, entire sub-dimensions may be irrelevant for certain industries, metric binning must reflect industry-specific distributions, and identical revenue trends can imply materially different risk profiles depending on industry, geography, and scale. An agentic scorecard allows the system to construct the evaluative framework itself - selecting relevant dimensions, defining appropriate metrics, choosing aggregation horizons, and interpreting patterns contextually - thereby aligning the structure of assessment with the economic reality of each individual business rather than forcing heterogeneous businesses into a rigid, one-size-fits-all schema.

## 4. Implications: Systems Whose Runtime Behavior Is Not Fully Known

A defining characteristic of a fully agentic, AI-native underwriting system is that *its exact runtime behavior cannot be exhaustively specified by its developers*.

This is not a flaw, but a design principle. Developers define agent roles, interaction protocols, learning boundaries, and governance constraints. But the specific reasoning paths, evaluative structures, and decision rationales emerge dynamically from data, context, and agent interaction. In this sense, underwriting systems begin to resemble institutional decision-making bodies rather than traditional software pipelines.

## 5. Conclusion

Deterministic scorecards are not obsolete; they are foundational. But in an AI-native future, they represent the bootstrapping layer for systems that reason, adapt, and evolve.

The long-term trajectory of credit underwriting is not toward better scores, but toward better assessments; assessments constructed, challenged, and revised by agents operating within carefully designed credit assessment frameworks.

In such a world, underwriting is no longer encoded; it is *continuously discovered*.

documents.019e2cc7-8f8d-7255-8997-314d4d7ccf22:
**Date:** 2026-02-18\
**Excerpt:** A 2-person MCA fund uses undersight to deliver evidence-backed underwriting on every deal, with full audit trails, document cross-referencing, and fraud detection.\
**Tag:** Case Study\
**Author:** Kyle Adriany

---

# How 4D Financing gets institutional-grade underwriting with a 2-person team

4D Financing is a direct MCA lender with a simple thesis: merchants deserve a better deal. Founded by Josh James, 4D operates with a lean team of two and a conviction that every funding decision should be backed by evidence, not gut instinct.

The challenge is obvious. Institutional-grade underwriting typically requires analysts, compliance officers, and manual review processes that don't scale for a small fund. 4D needed the rigor without the headcount.

## The problem: quality underwriting doesn't scale manually

Before undersight, 4D's underwriting process looked like most small funds: bank statements reviewed manually, financial documents cross-checked by hand, and risk assessments built on experience rather than systematic analysis.

This worked for a few deals per week. But it created three problems:

* **Inconsistency.** The same deal could get different treatment depending on the day, the workload, or the order in which documents were reviewed.
* **Missed signals.** Manual review catches obvious red flags but misses subtle patterns like revenue seasonality, processor changes, and deposit anomalies that only emerge when statements are analyzed systematically.
* **No audit trail.** When a deal goes bad, reconstructing why it was approved requires digging through emails and memory. There's no structured record of what was analyzed and what was concluded.

## How undersight changed the workflow

4D embedded undersight's AI underwriting agent into their deal flow. Every application now follows the same process:

### Step 1: Documents uploaded to undersight

Bank statements, tax returns, and financial documents are uploaded. undersight ingests and parses everything, with no manual data entry and no spreadsheet templates.

### Step 2: AI cross-references everything

The agent doesn't just read documents in isolation. It cross-references deposits against reported revenue, matches tax return figures to bank activity, and flags discrepancies. A merchant claiming $50K monthly revenue but showing $30K in deposits gets flagged automatically, with citations to the specific documents and line items.

### Step 3: Risk profile built systematically

The agent builds a comprehensive risk profile covering revenue volatility, concentration risk, seasonal patterns, and existing positions. Every data point traced back to its source. Every conclusion explained in plain language.

### Step 4: Terms proposed or decline issued, with reasoning

The agent doesn't just output a score. It proposes terms or recommends decline with a full explanation: here's what was analyzed, here's what was found, here's why this deal does or doesn't work. Josh reviews the reasoning and makes the final call.

## What changed

### Every decision is auditable

When a deal performs well, 4D knows exactly why it was approved. When one doesn't, they can trace the decision back to specific data points and reasoning. This isn't just good practice. It's the kind of documentation that institutional capital partners require.

### Fraud detection catches what humans miss

The cross-referencing engine flags patterns that manual review almost never catches: deposits from unexpected sources, revenue figures that don't match across documents, processing changes that suggest account instability. In 4D's first month on undersight, the agent flagged two applications with manufactured bank statements that would have passed visual inspection.

### Consistency at any volume

Whether 4D reviews 5 deals or 50 in a week, every application gets the same thorough analysis. The quality of underwriting is decoupled from the team's bandwidth.

### A 2-person team operates like a 20-person one

Josh still makes every funding decision. But instead of spending hours per deal on manual analysis, he reviews an AI-generated underwriting memo that already cross-checked the documents, identified the risks, and proposed terms. His expertise goes into judgment, not data processing.

## By the numbers

* **Minutes** to underwrite a deal (vs. hours manually)
* **100%** audit trail on every decision
* **3-way** document cross-check (bank statements, tax returns, financials)
* **24hr** funding after approval

## In Josh's words

> We started 4D because merchants deserve a better deal. undersight gives us the underwriting rigor of a large institution without the headcount. Every decision is backed by data, cross-referenced across documents, and fully auditable. That's how we keep our promise to merchants: we only fund deals that make sense for them.

## Why this matters

4D Financing represents the kind of fund that the MCA industry needs more of: principled, transparent, and rigorous. undersight makes that possible without requiring the infrastructure of a large institution.

The same AI underwriting agent that powers 4D is available to any fund, brokerage, or marketplace on the undersight platform. The technology is the same. The values are yours.

documents.019e2ddb-c38c-70e3-acce-f9383aea6adb:
# From application to funded deal

## Step 1: An applicant or broker starts a conversation

Macy, the AI intake agent, collects bank statements, answers questions, and resolves RFIs in a single chat session. No forms, no email chains.

## Step 2: Documents are enriched and verified

Raw data becomes structured intelligence. Revenue patterns, existing positions, and anomalies are identified and cross-referenced automatically.

## Step 3: Risk is scored in seconds

ML models trained on deal outcomes score the applicant across revenue patterns, existing positions, and anomaly signals. The risk score is returned in seconds.

## Step 4: The underwriter makes the call

AI surfaces what matters. Precedent lookup, pricing suggestions, and decline rationale are generated automatically. The underwriter still decides.

documents.019e3d4d-d4c0-72c0-9204-1a9637e0d266:
# Scoring as an API

The same engine powering the copilot, available as a low-friction endpoint. Send applicant data, receive a grade, metrics, and an array of insights. Try it before integrating the full platform.

## Positioning

We have a score, just like everyone else. If you want to take our score - if you don't want to use our full platform, just our score to test it out - here it is. The scoring engine combines an ML baseline with agentic adjustment: deterministic where the data is clean, adaptive where context matters.

## Example response

```
POST /v1/score
{
  "applicant_id": "...",
  "bank_statements": [...]
}

{
  "grade": "B+",
  "ml_baseline": 0.62,
  "agentic_adjustment": 0.22,
  "metrics": {
    "monthly_revenue": 184500,
    "revenue_volatility": 0.18,
    "existing_positions": 2
  },
  "insights": [
    "Revenue trend stable across last 6 months",
    "Two active positions identified, capacity remaining",
    "No anomalies in deposit patterns"
  ]
}
```

These JSON snippets are marketing - the response shape evolves with the engine. Contact us to discuss integration.

## CTA

**Heading:** Request scoring API access\
**Button:** Request access

# Why teams pick the copilot

A side-by-side of how undersight's copilot compares to manual underwriting and generic risk-scoring APIs. Eucalyptus checkmarks for "yes," red Xs for "no," neutral dashes for "partial."

## Matrix

| Feature | undersight Copilot | Manual Underwriting | Other Risk-Scoring APIs |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Agentic intake (Macy) for documents and RFIs | check | x | x |
| ML risk score in seconds | check | x | check |
| Agentic adjustment over ML baseline | check | x | x |
| Continuous portfolio monitoring | check | partial | x |
| Document enrichment + cross-reference | check | partial | x |
| Bank statement parsing (Plaid, MX, Yodlee, PDFs) | check | partial | partial |
| MCA-specific anomaly detection | check | partial | x |
| Underwriter-in-the-loop decisioning | check | check | x |
| Audit trail per decision | check | partial | x |
| Scales without adding headcount | check | x | check |

## Legend

* **check** -> fully supported (rendered as Eucalyptus check)
* **x** -> not supported (rendered as red X)
* **partial** -> limited or workaround (rendered as Cloud Zinc dash)

"secret": "019e1f07-df81-73d5-9051-50ca674c1a9d",\
"content": "**Date:** 2026-05-08\\\\n**Excerpt:** A deal auto-declined by rules-based underwriting gets funded in under 5 minutes through agentic intake and pre-underwriting.\\\\n**Tag:** Case Study\\n\\n---\\n\\n# How Chat Advance funded a declined deal in 5 minutes\\n\\nChat Advance is a funding marketplace built on top of undersight. When a traditional rules-based underwriter auto-declined a small business applicant, the undersight platform told a different story.\\n\\n## The setup\\n\\nA small business owner needed $30K for 60 days. They had reliable but lumpy revenue from state contracts. On paper, the deal didn't fit standard criteria. A rules-based system declined it instantly.\\n\\nBut Chat Advance runs on undersight. Instead of a static application form, the applicant connected with Macy, the AI intake agent.\\n\\n## What happened next\\n\\n### Step 1: The applicant engages via chat\\n\\nMacy guided the business owner through the process, answering questions about revenue-based financing along the way. No forms, no email chains. A single conversation.\\n\\n### Step 2: Bank data ingested and enriched\\n\\nThree months of bank statements were uploaded via Plaid. underchat enriched the data automatically. Transactions categorized, revenue patterns identified, anomalies flagged for verification.\\n\\n### Step 3: RFIs addressed with the applicant\\n\\nMacy identified potential underwriter questions about the bank statement anomalies and addressed them directly with the applicant. No human reviewer needed for the initial pass.\\n\\n### Step 4: Matching funders receive a deal submission\\n\\nFunders matching the deal criteria received a rich submission package including preliminary RFI responses and deal analysis. Two funders matched. Two offers extended.\\n\\n## The result\\n\\nThe applicant received two funding offers within five minutes of starting the conversation:\\n\\n\* Offer 1: $100K, 26 weeks, 1.25x factor\\n\* Offer 2: $80K, 34 weeks, 1.34x factor\\n\\nA deal that was auto-declined by conventional screening got funded because agentic intake captured what a rules engine couldn't see.\\n\\n## Why this matters\\n\\nThis is not about replacing underwriters. It's about creating a path for deals that would otherwise never get evaluated. The undersight stack handled intake, enrichment, RFI resolution, and funder matching end-to-end. The human-in-the-loop approved and closed.\\n\\n### By the numbers\\n\\n\* **24/7** availability for applicants\\n\* **Ultra-low** customer acquisition cost\\n\* **60-second** screening\\n\* **Personalized** direct engagement\\n\\nChat Advance is one example. The same agentic workflow runs for funds, brokers, and marketplaces across the undersight platform."\
}
documents.019e3299-c249-7089-9b99-c8c0db4f8a46:
{\
"secret": "019e27cc-5434-73d2-81c2-6ba4bdce647b",\
"content": "**Date:** 2026-01-22\\\\n**Excerpt:** How agentic AI architectures can transform credit assessment from rigid scorecards to adaptive, context-aware evaluation systems.\\\\n**Tag:** Research\\\\n**Author:** Sajit Roshan\\\\n**Subtitle:** A Vision for AI-Native Credit Assessment Architectures\\n\\n---\\n\\n# From Deterministic Scorecards to Agentic Credit Assessments\\n\\n## A Vision for AI-Native Credit Assessment Architectures\\n\\n### Abstract\\n\\nTraditional small-business credit underwriting assessments rely on deterministic scorecards: fixed hierarchies of dimensions, metrics, bins, and weights designed ex ante by domain experts. While effective for standardization and governance, such systems fundamentally assume that risk is enumerable, stationary, and linearly aggregable. Such systems also assume that a single scorecard (or a small-set of similar scorecards) can cover all types and sizes of businesses. This paper outlines a research and engineering vision for transitioning from deterministic scorecards to fully agentic, AI-native credit assessments; systems in which not only credit decisions, but the very *structure of evaluation itself*, emerges dynamically at runtime.\\n\\n## 1. The Deterministic Scorecard as a Necessary but Insufficient Baseline\\n\\nDeterministic scorecards represent a critical starting point. They encode institutional knowledge, regulatory constraints, and operational consistency through:\\n\\n\* Pre-defined dimensions (e.g., revenue quality, cash flow strength)\\n\* Fixed sub-dimensions (e.g., stability, durability, concentration)\\n\* Deterministic metrics, bins, scores, and hierarchical weights\\n\* Aggregation rules yielding interpretable ratings at each level\\n\\nThis framework establishes a shared ontology of risk, enabling reproducibility, auditability, and alignment across teams. However, this approach also hard-codes assumptions about what constitutes "risk", how risk factors interact, and which signals matter across ALL businesses and regimes. As a result, deterministic scorecards are inherently brittle in the face of the long tail of small businesses, non-stationary environments, and adversarial or strategic behavior.\\n\\n## 2. Introducing Agentic Assessment Layers Over Deterministic Structure\\n\\nThe first evolutionary step is not replacement, but augmentation. We envision agentic layers that *operate over* the deterministic scorecard, producing structured assessments at both the sub-dimension and dimension levels. These assessments are not mere scores, but reasoned judgments, expressed as a rating, a short natural-language justification, and an associated confidence or uncertainty measure.\\n\\nTwo modes of agentic assessment are possible:\\n\\n### 2.1 Semi-Agentic Assessment\\n\\nIn this mode, the agent has access to the deterministic scorecard's metrics, binning logic, metric scores, weights and aggregation rules exposed via programmatic interfaces (e.g., MCP-style tools). The agent reasons *within* the scorecard's structure while adding interpretation, contextualization, and cross-metric synthesis. This allows the system to retain strong governance while introducing flexibility and explanation.\\n\\n### 2.2 Fully-Agentic Assessment\\n\\nIn contrast, fully-agentic assessment removes reliance on predefined metrics, bins and weights. The agent forms independent judgments about pre-defined sub-dimensions and dimensions based on observed evidence, learned patterns, and contextual knowledge. The deterministic scorecard becomes a reference point rather than an execution constraint.\\n\\nThis marks the transition from *rule-following evaluation* to *model-based reasoning*.\\n\\nIntroducing agentic assessment layers over deterministic scorecards naturally enables agent specialization and composable decision architectures that are impossible in monolithic scoring systems. Rather than a single model producing a unified score, multiple specialized agents can be assigned distinct cognitive roles: domain-expert underwriting agents focusing on specific scorecard dimensions (e.g., revenue behavior, leverage, cash-flow resilience), agents dedicated to metric interpretation (such as binning or normalization), and agents responsible for weighting, aggregation, or uncertainty estimation. These agents can be organized in different architectural patterns such as a risk-committee configuration, where two or three expert underwriting agents deliberate in parallel and a deliberately contrarian agent challenges consensus assumptions; or a sequential expert pipeline, where reasoning is progressively refined and stress-tested across stages. Such agentic architectures mirror real-world credit committees while remaining fully programmable, allowing institutions to encode diversity of perspective, adversarial reasoning, and domain specialization directly into the underwriting system, rather than relying on a single aggregated model or opaque score.\\n\\n## 3. Agentic Construction of the Scorecard Itself\\n\\nThe next - and more radical - stage is to treat the scorecard not as a static artifact, but as a *generated object*. Under this paradigm dimensions, sub-dimensions, and metrics are not fixed ex ante. Each deal results in a *bespoke evaluative structure*, and the "scorecard" becomes an emergent representation of how the system understands that specific business as well as the quality and the quantity of data available for underwriting.\\n\\nHere, underwriting is no longer the act of populating a predefined schema. Instead, it is the act of both constructing an appropriate scorecard as well as conducting a credit assessment using that carefully crafted scorecard.\\n\\nAn agentic construction of the scorecard is not a nicety, nor an attempt to force agentic thinking onto an existing framework. It is a necessity in small-business credit assessment because the underlying evaluation problem is inherently non-uniform and context-dependent. Small businesses vary dramatically in size, industry, operating cadence, and data exhaust - ranging from short bank statement histories to multi-year records, and from clean financials to partial or idiosyncratic documents such as AR-aging reports or irregular statements. In such settings, pre-defining deterministic scorecards for all permutations is neither practical nor epistemically sound: even basic statistical constructs (e.g., standard deviation) may be meaningless given limited history, while aggregation choices (daily vs. weekly vs. monthly inflows) must adapt to data length and business rhythm. Moreover, entire sub-dimensions may be irrelevant for certain industries, metric binning must reflect industry-specific distributions, and identical revenue trends can imply materially different risk profiles depending on industry, geography, and scale. An agentic scorecard allows the system to construct the evaluative framework itself - selecting relevant dimensions, defining appropriate metrics, choosing aggregation horizons, and interpreting patterns contextually - thereby aligning the structure of assessment with the economic reality of each individual business rather than forcing heterogeneous businesses into a rigid, one-size-fits-all schema.\\n\\n## 4. Implications: Systems Whose Runtime Behavior Is Not Fully Known\\n\\nA defining characteristic of a fully agentic, AI-native underwriting system is that *its exact runtime behavior cannot be exhaustively specified by its developers*.\\n\\nThis is not a flaw, but a design principle. Developers define agent roles, interaction protocols, learning boundaries, and governance constraints. But the specific reasoning paths, evaluative structures, and decision rationales emerge dynamically from data, context, and agent interaction. In this sense, underwriting systems begin to resemble institutional decision-making bodies rather than traditional software pipelines.\\n\\n## 5. Conclusion\\n\\nDeterministic scorecards are not obsolete; they are foundational. But in an AI-native future, they represent the bootstrapping layer for systems that reason, adapt, and evolve.\\n\\nThe long-term trajectory of credit underwriting is not toward better scores, but toward better assessments; assessments constructed, challenged, and revised by agents operating within carefully designed credit assessment frameworks.\\n\\nIn such a world, underwriting is no longer encoded; it is *continuously discovered*."\
}
documents.019e3299-c249-708b-83df-31b26a0b23a9:
{\
"secret": "019e22a9-2522-7174-9582-18beb1996be5",\
"content": "**Date:** 2025-12-12\\\\n**Excerpt:** A technology-enabled transformation thesis for how undersight helps C/D-rated funds achieve ABS-grade standards and access institutional capital.\\\\n**Tag:** Research\\\\n**Author:** Kyle Adriany\\\\n**Subtitle:** A Technology-Enabled Transformation Thesis\\n\\n---\\n\\n# Unlocking Institutional Capital for Mid-Tier MCA Funds\\n\\n*A Technology-Enabled Transformation Thesis*\\n\\n**How undersight enables C/D-rated funds to achieve ABS-grade standards**\\n\\n*For investors and bank CROs evaluating the MCA credit opportunity*\\n\\nThis analysis builds on [Edgar Matthews & Co., "The Institutional Migration of Merchant Cash Advance and Revenue-Based Finance," January 2026](https://www.edgarmatthews.com).\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Executive Summary\\n\\nMCA securitization has matured into a credible institutional asset class. Since 2021, approximately eight platforms have successfully executed rated securitizations, collectively securitizing over $1.1 billion in 2025 alone. These platforms include [Enova (OnDeck)](https://www.enova.com), [Kapitus](https://www.kapitus.com), and [BasePoint](https://www.basepointcapital.com).\\n\\nHowever, the vast majority of MCA funds remain structurally locked out of this capital. The barrier is not asset quality or borrower demand. The central limitation is the absence of institutional-grade structure across data, legal enforceability, servicing infrastructure, and portfolio governance.\\n\\nThis represents a significant market dislocation. Institutional capital seeks high-yield access within the $1.34 trillion US private credit market, yet most MCA originators cannot meet the operational standards required for warehouse facilities, let alone rated securitization.\\n\\n## The Transformation Opportunity\\n\\nTechnology can close this gap. Mid-tier funds that modernize their operational infrastructure along five institutional dimensions can transform from uninvestible to attractive. The Matthews framework identifies these dimensions as:\\n\\n| Dimension | Weight | Standard |\\n| --- | --- | --- |\\n| Data Integrity | 25% | Immutable contracts, clean reconciliation, historical depth |\\n| Payment Stability | 25% | Automated collections, explainable patterns |\\n| Servicing Continuity | 20% | Documented processes, personnel resilience |\\n| Buyback Discipline | 15% | Clear triggers, adequate funding |\\n| Stacking Controls | 15% | Detection capability, performance differentiation |\\n\\nundersight is building the infrastructure that enables this transformation. By embedding autonomous underwriting and portfolio monitoring technology, undersight supports four of five dimensions directly.\\n\\n### Key Market Indicators\\n\\n\* $613M securitized across three 2025 deals ([ONDK](https://www.enova.com), [Kapitus](https://www.kapitus.com), [BasePoint](https://www.basepointcapital.com))\\n\* 21+ total MCA securitizations since 2021\\n\* Class A tranche pricing: T+125 to T+232\\n\* \\\~8 unique sponsors have achieved institutional access\\n\\nThe question is not whether MCA assets are institutionally viable. The question is how to scale that viability beyond the handful of platforms that have self-funded the transformation.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## What Disqualifies Mid-Tier Funds Today\\n\\nThe deficiencies are operational, not economic. Funds fail institutional standards not because assets underperform, but because infrastructure cannot withstand diligence. Key structural weaknesses:\\n\\n**Legal uncertainty:** Many MCA structures rely on sale-of-receivables doctrine to avoid usury statutes, but courts increasingly apply "function-over-form" analysis. Platforms must be engineered to survive loan re-characterization rather than relying on sale doctrine as a sole line of defense.\\n\\n**Enforcement fragility:** In MCA, collateral is operational enforcement. The ability to identify disruption, implement remedies, and restore remittance determines recovery more than contractual documentation. Funds without scalable enforcement infrastructure cannot support institutional leverage.\\n\\n**Informational opacity:** Without granular disruption data, recovery timing, and re-engagement rates, rating agencies cannot differentiate between recoverable operational issues and terminal credit losses.\\n\\n**Stacking correlation:** When multiple funders underwrite the same revenue stream without centralized coordination, competing daily pulls fragment cash flow across all exposures. Stacking converts portfolio granularity into hidden correlation, rendering institutional tranching mathematically unstable.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Market Potential: Latent Demand for High-Yield Access\\n\\n### The Yield Arbitrage\\n\\nMCA assets offer exceptional risk-adjusted returns. Net yields after losses range from 18-25% for well-managed portfolios; institutional private credit targeting small business lending typically achieves 8-12%.\\n\\nThis spread reflects operational intensity and structural complexity, not market inefficiency. For institutional capital willing to deploy through qualifying platforms, the risk-adjusted opportunity is substantial.\\n\\nRecent securitization pricing demonstrates appetite. Class A tranches in 2025 deals priced between T+125 and T+232:\\n\\n\* ONDK 2025-2 (AA rated): T+125, $261M\\n\* BSPNT 2025-1 (A/A- rated): T+232, $102M\\n\* KAPT 2025-1 (AA- rated): T+185, $250M\\n\\n### Why Banks Cannot Lend Directly to C/D Funds\\n\\nBanks face a structural dilemma. High-yield MCA assets fit portfolio allocation mandates, but most originators cannot satisfy credit committee requirements:\\n\\n1. **Data insufficiency:** Banks require loan-level historical data, reconciliation trails, and static pool analytics. Most mid-tier funds cannot produce this consistently.\\n2. **Servicing scalability:** Warehouse facilities require third-party servicer ratings or demonstrable operational resilience. Key-person dependencies disqualify platforms.\\n3. **Legal uncertainty:** Function-over-form risk creates regulatory capital complications.\\n4. **Stacking correlation:** Without cross-platform visibility, banks cannot assess true portfolio risk.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## How undersight Enables the Transformation\\n\\n### Positioning: Infrastructure, Not Software\\n\\nundersight does not just sell software to MCA funds. undersight creates the infrastructure that makes institutional capital deployment possible.\\n\\nThe positioning is narrow by design. undersight sits between the fund and the bank, addressing the specific structural deficiencies that prevent capital flow.\\n\\n### The Three-Layer Value Stack\\n\\nundersight organizes capabilities into three progressive layers:\\n\\n**Layer 1: Automate**\\n\\n\* Autonomous underwriting using agentic AI\\n\* Replaces manual document processing, credit data aggregation, deal structuring\\n\* Usage-based pricing scaling with deal volume\\n\* \\\~$6B addressable market across small business underwriting\\n\\n**Layer 2: Monitor**\\n\\n\* Continuous portfolio monitoring, payment pattern analysis, disruption detection\\n\* Early warning systems, downside protection\\n\* Progressing toward outcome-based pricing\\n\* \\\~$0.5-1.5B addressable market\\n\\n**Layer 3: Unlock**\\n\\n\* Audit-ready reporting, structured data exports, compliance frameworks\\n\* Institutional observability, capital enablement\\n\* \\\~$0.5-1.0B addressable market\\n\\nThe progression is deliberate. Funds adopt Layer 1 for efficiency. Layer 2 compounds value through performance gains. Layer 3 unlocks the transformation payoff: institutional capital access at materially better terms.\\n\\n### Mapping to Matthews Dimensions\\n\\n| Dimension | undersight Layer | Gap Closed |\\n| --- | --- | --- |\\n| Data Integrity (25%) | Layer 1: Automate | Complete. Immutable, structured origination data at deal inception |\\n| Payment Stability (25%) | Layer 2: Monitor | Substantial. Distinguishes business failure from cash-flow disruption |\\n| Servicing Continuity (20%) | Layer 1: Automate | Complete. Documented, system-driven workflows |\\n| Buyback Discipline (15%) | Layer 2: Monitor | Complete. Early default prediction, trigger monitoring |\\n| Stacking Controls (15%) | Layers 1 + 2 | Substantial. Position detection plus ongoing monitoring |\\n\\nundersight addresses four of five dimensions directly and provides substantial support for the fifth (Payment Stability requires complementary operational improvements that technology alone cannot fully solve).\\n\\n### Why Agentic AI Matters\\n\\nTraditional underwriting automation uses rules-based systems or ML models. undersight employs agentic AI with tool access operating within structured workflows. This architecture directly enables institutional readiness:\\n\\n\* **Immutability:** Agentic systems generate audit trails by design. Every decision includes reasoning, data sources, and confidence scoring.\\n\* **Pattern recognition:** Diverse signals (processor changes, bank account updates, geographic patterns) enable accurate disruption classification.\\n\* **Process codification:** The agent's reasoning becomes documentation, eliminating key-person dependencies.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## What Needs to Align: The Three-Party Coordination Problem\\n\\nTransformation requires alignment across the fund, the bank, and the infrastructure provider. The thesis succeeds only when all three coordinate.\\n\\n### Fund-Side Requirements\\n\\n**Profile:** Small/mid-tier MCA fund ($25-250M annual originations) seeking competitive differentiation.\\n\\n**Leadership commitment:** Buy-in for organizational transformation, not merely cost reduction.\\n\\n**Timeline:** 12-24 month transformation horizon. Data integrity improvements begin immediately; servicing continuity matures over 6-12 months; institutional pitch-readiness emerges in year two.\\n\\n### Bank-Side Requirements\\n\\n**Sponsor:** CRO or senior credit officer who defines "institutional-grade" for deployment criteria.\\n\\n**Engagement:** Feedback during transformation, not pass/fail evaluation at the end.\\n\\n**The carrot:** Defined capital deployment structure, whether warehouse facility, SPV participation, or direct ABS investment.\\n\\n### undersight Role\\n\\n**Not:** A software vendor selling tools.\\n\\n**Instead:** Infrastructure partner enabling transformation through deep operational integration. The infrastructure must become the operational foundation.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Conclusion\\n\\n### For Investors\\n\\nundersight addresses a market dislocation at the intersection of operational infrastructure and institutional capital access.\\n\\n**The opportunity:** MCA securitization is proven viable. However, the majority of funds remain locked out despite originating attractive yield assets. The first platform to enable transformation at scale captures capital concentration from both fund and bank sides.\\n\\n**Market size:** $7-8.5B across three layers.\\n\\n**Traction:** Founded October 2025, product launched November 2025, closed first customer at $100K ARR in December 2025.\\n\\n### For Banks\\n\\nMid-tier MCA funds are underserved, not uninvestible.\\n\\n**The framework:** [Matthews' five-dimension readiness index](https://www.edgarmatthews.com) provides the evaluation rubric.\\n\\n**The infrastructure layer:** Technology can close the operational gap. Funds that embed undersight infrastructure generate documentation, analytics, and process evidence that credit committees require.\\n\\n### Closing\\n\\nThe institutionalization of MCA will not be broad. But for the few platforms that execute correctly, the payoff will be transformational.\\n\\nundersight is building the infrastructure that makes that execution possible.\\n\\n---\\n\\n## Market Size\\n\\n**US MCA Market: $19-22B annual originations | 7-9% CAGR (2024-2032)**\\n\\n| Tier | Originations | Funders |\\n| --- | --- | --- |\\n| Enterprise ($1B+) | $8-12B | 5-10 |\\n| Large ($250M-$1B) | $5-8B | 10-20 |\\n| Mid-Market ($50M-$250M) | $3-6B | 30-50 |\\n| Small ($10M-$50M) | $1.5-3B | 50-100 |\\n| Micro (<$10M) | $0.5-1B | 100-150 |\\n| **Total** | **$19-22B** | **200-300** |\\n\\n\* 1,000-2,000 ISOs/brokers\\n\\n*Working Paper, Draft 0.1. Author: Kyle Adriany, undersight.*"\
}
documents.019e3299-c249-708d-a7bf-1c49dd28f094:
{\
"secret": "019e2cc7-8f8d-7255-8997-314d4d7ccf22",\
"content": "**Date:** 2026-02-18\\\\n**Excerpt:** A 2-person MCA fund uses undersight to deliver evidence-backed underwriting on every deal \\u2014 with full audit trails, document cross-referencing, and fraud detection.\\\\n**Tag:** Case Study\\\\n**Author:** Kyle Adriany\\n\\n---\\n\\n# How 4D Financing gets institutional-grade underwriting with a 2-person team\\n\\n4D Financing is a direct MCA lender with a simple thesis: merchants deserve a better deal. Founded by Josh James, 4D operates with a lean team of two and a conviction that every funding decision should be backed by evidence, not gut instinct.\\n\\nThe challenge is obvious. Institutional-grade underwriting typically requires analysts, compliance officers, and manual review processes that don't scale for a small fund. 4D needed the rigor without the headcount.\\n\\n## The problem: quality underwriting doesn't scale manually\\n\\nBefore undersight, 4D's underwriting process looked like most small funds: bank statements reviewed manually, financial documents cross-checked by hand, and risk assessments built on experience rather than systematic analysis.\\n\\nThis worked for a few deals per week. But it created three problems:\\n\\n\* **Inconsistency.** The same deal could get different treatment depending on the day, the workload, or the order in which documents were reviewed.\\n\* **Missed signals.** Manual review catches obvious red flags but misses subtle patterns \\u2014 revenue seasonality, processor changes, deposit anomalies that only emerge when statements are analyzed systematically.\\n\* **No audit trail.** When a deal goes bad, reconstructing why it was approved requires digging through emails and memory. There's no structured record of what was analyzed and what was concluded.\\n\\n## How undersight changed the workflow\\n\\n4D embedded undersight's AI underwriting agent into their deal flow. Every application now follows the same process:\\n\\n### Step 1: Documents uploaded to undersight\\n\\nBank statements, tax returns, and financial documents are uploaded. undersight ingests and parses everything \\u2014 no manual data entry, no spreadsheet templates.\\n\\n### Step 2: AI cross-references everything\\n\\nThe agent doesn't just read documents in isolation. It cross-references deposits against reported revenue, matches tax return figures to bank activity, and flags discrepancies. A merchant claiming $50K monthly revenue but showing $30K in deposits gets flagged automatically \\u2014 with citations to the specific documents and line items.\\n\\n### Step 3: Risk profile built systematically\\n\\nRevenue volatility, concentration risk, seasonal patterns, existing positions \\u2014 the agent builds a comprehensive risk profile. Every data point traced back to its source. Every conclusion explained in plain language.\\n\\n### Step 4: Terms proposed or decline issued \\u2014 with reasoning\\n\\nThe agent doesn't just output a score. It proposes terms or recommends decline with a full explanation: here's what was analyzed, here's what was found, here's why this deal does or doesn't work. Josh reviews the reasoning and makes the final call.\\n\\n## What changed\\n\\n### Every decision is auditable\\n\\nWhen a deal performs well, 4D knows exactly why it was approved. When one doesn't, they can trace the decision back to specific data points and reasoning. This isn't just good practice \\u2014 it's the kind of documentation that institutional capital partners require.\\n\\n### Fraud detection catches what humans miss\\n\\nThe cross-referencing engine flags patterns that manual review almost never catches: deposits from unexpected sources, revenue figures that don't match across documents, processing changes that suggest account instability. In 4D's first month on undersight, the agent flagged two applications with manufactured bank statements that would have passed visual inspection.\\n\\n### Consistency at any volume\\n\\nWhether 4D reviews 5 deals or 50 in a week, every application gets the same thorough analysis. The quality of underwriting is decoupled from the team's bandwidth.\\n\\n### A 2-person team operates like a 20-person one\\n\\nJosh still makes every funding decision. But instead of spending hours per deal on manual analysis, he reviews an AI-generated underwriting memo that already cross-checked the documents, identified the risks, and proposed terms. His expertise goes into judgment, not data processing.\\n\\n## By the numbers\\n\\n\* **Minutes** to underwrite a deal (vs. hours manually)\\n\* **100%** audit trail on every decision\\n\* **3-way** document cross-check (bank statements, tax returns, financials)\\n\* **24hr** funding after approval\\n\\n## In Josh's words\\n\\n> We started 4D because merchants deserve a better deal. undersight gives us the underwriting rigor of a large institution without the headcount. Every decision is backed by data, cross-referenced across documents, and fully auditable. That's how we keep our promise to merchants \\u2014 we only fund deals that make sense for them.\\n\\n## Why this matters\\n\\n4D Financing represents the kind of fund that the MCA industry needs more of: principled, transparent, and rigorous. undersight makes that possible without requiring the infrastructure of a large institution.\\n\\nThe same AI underwriting agent that powers 4D is available to any fund, brokerage, or marketplace on the undersight platform. The technology is the same. The values are yours."\
}
documents.019e3299-c249-708f-ba68-411efe759e3f:
{\
"secret": "019e1de0-9dbc-70af-9728-5dd3849152a7",\
"content": "**Date:** 2025-11-20\\\\n**Excerpt:** The role of AI in private credit is augmentation, not automation.\\\\n**Tag:** Insight\\\\n**Author:** Sajit Roshan\\n\\n---\\n\\nThe conversation around AI in underwriting often gets framed as replacement. Will machines take over? The answer is no, and here is why.\\n\\nPrivate credit underwriting requires judgment that cannot be reduced to a formula. What AI does is remove the drudge work: data gathering, initial screening, precedent lookup. The underwriter still makes the call.\\n\\nThe best analogy is spell-check for writers. No one argues that spell-check replaced authors. It freed them to focus on craft. AI underwriting tools do the same for risk professionals.\\n\\nundersight built underchat with this philosophy. Every feature exists to make the underwriter faster and more consistent, not to replace their judgment. The copilot surfaces what matters. The human decides."\
}
documents.019e3299-c249-7091-8ab4-02d4f60b847f:
{\
"secret": "019e1de0-aa70-72e2-a186-f94e95fde206",\
"content": "**Date:** 2026-02-18\\\\n**Excerpt:** Client intake is the slowest link in the submission chain. Here is how to fix it.\\\\n**Tag:** Insight\\\\n**Author:** Sajit Roshan\\n\\n---\\n\\nEvery funder knows the pain: incomplete submissions, back-and-forth emails, missing bank statements. The RFI process is where deals stall and brokers lose patience.\\n\\nAgentic Client RFI attacks this directly. Instead of static forms and email chains, an AI agent conducts an intelligent conversation with the broker, gathering exactly what is needed, validating as it goes, and producing a clean structured submission.\\n\\nThe result: funder-ready packages assembled from raw inputs. No manual follow-up. Zero back-and-forth. Brokers get a better experience, and underwriters get complete files."\
}
documents.019e3299-c249-7093-b165-a5c219b30550:
{\
"secret": "019e1de0-b95d-71f6-aab6-aecc7c21e347",\
"content": "**Date:** 2026-03-12\\\\n**Excerpt:** Lessons learned building AI assistants for institutional underwriting teams.\\\\n**Tag:** Insight\\\\n**Author:** Sajit Roshan\\n\\n---\\n\\nBuilding a copilot for underwriters is not the same as building a chatbot. Underwriters need precision, not conversation. They need relevant precedents surfaced at the right moment, not generic summaries.\\n\\nunderchat Co-pilot was designed around the actual workflow: review submission, assess exposure, check precedent, price, decide. Every feature maps to a real step in that process."\
}
